summary: Shapiro shares a simple woodworking project: handmade children's building blocks from scrap hardwood. He walks through the quick process of gluing, cutting, sanding, and finishing blocks from maple and walnut scraps. The post frames the project as an ideal gift for baby showers—something that looks impressive and can't be bought in stores, yet requires minimal effort from anyone with basic shop tools. It's a practical maker tip wrapped in the satisfaction of turning scraps into heirlooms.
key_points: The best handmade gifts require few operations but look expensive or can't be purchased
Children's building blocks can be made from shop scraps in about two hours
Safety consideration: blocks must be larger than mouth-sized to be safe for babies
Simple finishing with shellac or nontoxic natural oils makes scrap wood into a polished gift
money_quotes: One of the good things about being a woodworker is that you can whip out great gifts with very little work.
The trick is to pick the gifts–you want stuff that requires very few operations on the machines, but looks cool and either is very expensive or can't be bought.
I did a lousy job of shellacking them, but baby won't care, right?
Try making a set for your next baby shower. You'll thank me for it.
Let me know if you need a lumber hookup in the Seattle area.
key_insight: The best handmade gifts maximize the gap between perceived value and actual effort—simple woodworking techniques turn shop scraps into heirloom-quality children's toys.
